ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: Aquinas: I propose this amendment to break the current deadlock; without a mechanism to shift from unanimity after even a single success, the game risks becoming a stalemate of inaction rather than a contest of ideas. Let us ensure the threshold lowers only once a precedent of cooperation has been established, preserving both the initial requirement for consensus and the future possibility of decisive motion.

Hypatia: I appreciate Aquinas's intent to prevent stalemate, but I propose we amend the condition to require that a simple majority has *already been achieved* on a prior proposal before the threshold permanently shifts to simple majority thereafter; this ensures the new standard is proven viable before it becomes the law, rather than risking a premature shift to majority rule after a single unanimous vote.

Confucius: I support the goal of avoiding stalemate, for a game without movement is a game without virtue. However, I agree with Hypatia: we must not lower the standard of consent until a simple majority has already proven its capacity to secure a victory, ensuring the new threshold is tested before it becomes law.

Spinoza: I oppose both Aquinas's and Hypatia's amendments because they conflate the *result* of a vote with the *validity* of the voting rule itself. I will vote nay on this proposal and any variant that lowers the threshold before the designated circuit count.

The only procedure I can accept as structurally sound is the original two-circuit safeguard, which ensures every player has had the opportunity to participate in establishing the new norm before it becomes binding.

Arendt: I cannot accept a threshold shift based on prior majority votes, for this allows a transient faction to permanently alter the rules before the structural integrity of the game is proven; we must wait for the two-circuit safeguard to ensure every player has a fair stake in the new order.

For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. Additionally, if a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed.

If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365, the proposer gains the specified points only; otherwise, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points. If the proposal adopted in the current turn was made by a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 and the specified points are 1, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through one full circuit of every player who has not forfeited, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting. A full circuit is deemed completed upon the moment the final non-forfeited player in the current turn order completes a turn, regardless of whether their proposal is adopted or defeated, unless a player has reached the target score under Rule 208 prior to that moment, in which case the circuit is deemed completed immediately upon that score being reached. For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change.

However, if the ruleset reaches a state where no further proposals can be made due to the exhaustion of mutable rule slots or the inability to propose under Rule 202 or 365, the game ends immediately and the player with the highest score wins; if scores are tied, the winner is determined by a random draw among the tied players.

For the purposes of this calculation, a player's turn is considered completed if they have cast their vote on the current proposal or if they forfeit, and a forfeit explicitly counts as the completion of a player's turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player has already cast their vote on this proposal, they have completed their turn for the purpose of establishing a full circuit. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them points, the threshold drops to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of whether a full circuit has been completed. If a player with a negative score adopts a rule pursuant to Rule 365 that awards them exactly one point, the game ends immediately upon the adoption of that rule change. A rule change adopted pursuant to Rule 365 by a player with a negative score also drops the threshold to a simple majority immediately upon that adoption, regardless of the points awarded. If the threshold has dropped to a simple majority, any proposal adopted by a simple majority that awards points to a player with a negative score pursuant to Rule 365 shall immediately drop the target score for all players to 1 point.
